text: Robotic transgastric cyst gastrostomy and pancreatic debridement.

Patient presented is a fifty-five-year-old female with history of severe acute pancreatitis of biliary etiology. She had undergone interval cholecystectomy but developed recurrent severe pancreatitis and ultimately a symptomatic retrogastric collection requiring gastrojejunostomy tube placement.

CT imaging demonstrated a cystic lesion replacing the body of the pancreas in close apposition to the stomach. There was cystic replacement of the body of the pancreas with residual enhancing tissue in the tail. There is heterogeneous material noted throughout the collection indicating that this is walled off pancreatic necrosis rather than a simple pseudocyst.
The patient was taken to the operating room for robotic cyst gastrostomy and pancreatic debridement.

The surgeon establishes initial access at the umbilicus using a 12mm robotic port, followed by the placement of three additional ports targeting the epigastrium. The assistant port and first robotic port are positioned in the midclavicular line, with the assistant port placed caudally to minimize instrument collisions.
After placing the trochars, the surgeon guides the robotic instruments into the abdomen under laparoscopic visualization.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic instruments to carefully dissect and remove adhesions from the previous gastrojejunostomy tube site within the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon elevates the stomach and performs an anterior gastrotomy using monopolar electrocautery, cutting through the stomach layers until the mucosa is visible. The gastrotomy incision is then extended further.
A large, blottable protuberance is observed on the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to identify and characterize a collection within the surgical field, providing real-time imaging of the area.
The surgeon enters the cavity using monopolar electrocautery, evacuating approximately two liters of fluid from the collection within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon extends the gastrotomy incision in the stomach to facilitate further debridement, revealing additional layers of gastric tissue.
The surgeon removes necrotic tissue from the cavity, preparing it to be extracted from the abdomen at the conclusion of the procedure.

This necrotic tissue is quite extensive, making endoscopic debridement quite difficult for walled off pancreatic necrosis. Therefore, our practice is to treat walled off pancreatic necrosis with minimally invasive surgical debridement to allow for an adequate debridement and drainage in a single minimally invasive procedure.
Small amounts of necrotic tissue, densely adherent to the cavity wall, are left in place. These tissues are expected to undergo auto-debridement facilitated by gastric secretions.

The surgeon matures the cyst gastrostomy by suturing the stomach to the cavity wall using resorbable barbed sutures in a running fashion. Two running sutures are anchored at the apices of the cyst gastrostomy.
The surgeon closes the anterior gastrotomy in a single layer using a resorbable barbed suture, taking full thickness bites of the gastric wall after the cyst gastrostomy is matured.

The surgeon places necrotic tissue into an endoscopic retrieval bag for removal from the abdomen. Options for ending the suture include tying the tails together using a locking plastic clip or running the sutures past the mid portion of the closure.
The surgeon reinforces the gastrotomy closure using aerosolized fiber and glue, ensuring a secure seal without placing any drains.

The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged on post-operative day five. She had resolution of her symptoms and post-operative CT scan demonstrated resolution of her retrogastric fluid collection.
